name: Stat card component (.stat-card)
description: Dashboard template hand-builds KPI/stat cards with label, big number, and change tag. Common dashboard pattern. Add a dedicated .stat-card component with proper structure and visual polish.

name: Add mobile app shell layout (iOS-safe)
description:
Create a mobile-first app shell layout that works perfectly on iOS Safari/Chrome. Requirements:
- Respect safe-area insets (top/bottom) for notch/home indicator
- Avoid 100vh issues (use dvh/svh or CSS-only strategy)
- Sticky top/bottom bars without content jump
- Main content scrolls (no double scroll)
- Works when URL bar hides/shows
- Compatible with light/dark themes
- Provide a demo and docs example
Acceptance: no layout glitches on modern iOS Safari.
result: Added .app-shell layout with grid-template-rows: auto 1fr auto, safe area variables (--safe-top/bottom/left/right using env()), sticky header/footer. Uses 100dvh for iOS URL bar issues. Added demo in Mobile.svelte.

name: 7. Create dynamic route page for /base
description:
## What Replace src/routes/(docs)/base/+page.svelte with a dynamic page that renders topics from .md files. ## Why This is the first route migration. We're doing one route at a time to catch issues early. ## Implementation ### Step 1: Save baseline ```bash curl -s -H 'Accept: text/markdown' http://localhost:6124/base > /tmp/base-agent-before.md ``` ### Step 2: Create new page Replace src/routes/(docs)/base/+page.svelte: ```svelte

Base

Foundation styles and variables.

There is a deep base of classless CSS defaults in Graffiti giving you good defaults without having to memorize a bunch of classes...

{#each topics as topic (topic.id)} {/each} ``` ### Step 3: Verify agent markdown unchanged ```bash curl -s -H 'Accept: text/markdown' http://localhost:6124/base > /tmp/base-agent-after.md diff /tmp/base-agent-before.md /tmp/base-agent-after.md ``` ### Step 4: Visual verification - Open http://localhost:6124/base in browser - Compare visually to current site - Check: All headings present? All demos render? Code highlighting works? ## Current Sections in base/+page.svelte 1. Typography (h1-h6, paragraph) 2. Variables & Tokens (spacing, radius, border, padding, line-height, shadows) 3. Colors (color scales, theming variables) 4. Dividers (hr) 5. Forms (text inputs, checkboxes, radios, date/time, range, file, validation) Each section should map to a topic in src/docs/content/topics/base-*.md ## Verification Checklist - [ ] All sections render - [ ] All demos show live preview + code - [ ] Deep links work (#typography, #variables, #colors, #forms) - [ ] Agent markdown identical before/after - [ ] pnpm build passes ## Files - REPLACE: src/routes/(docs)/base/+page.svelte
result: Replaced src/routes/(docs)/base/+page.svelte with runtime-driven rendering using getRouteIntro('base') and getTopicsForRoute('base'), then rendered each topic through TopicSection. Verified agent markdown parity with before/after curl + diff (no differences), ran npm run check:docs-content and npm run build, and validated in agent-browser on /base (topic anchors present, demos render, no error overlay).

name: Align spacing scale tokens

name: Phase 2: Improve Graffiti templates and library ergonomics from skill findings
description:
## Goal
Fix the template and library shortcomings surfaced in Phase 1 so Graffiti is easier for both humans and AI to use correctly.

## Why
Current templates demonstrate capability but contain significant inline-style debt and inconsistent composition patterns, which weakens AI reliability and user copy-paste outcomes.

## Scope
- Reduce template inline style debt with reusable class-based primitives
- Add missing utilities and variants in drop-in.css where recurring gaps exist
- Restore and harden AI chat template layout behavior
- Align llms docs with canonical template usage
- Add quality guardrails to prevent regressions
- Audit every template and key feature cluster at mobile breakpoints

## Deliverables
- Refactored templates in src/routes/templates/
- Library improvements in src/lib/drop-in.css (and related docs/demo updates)
- Updated llms guidance reflecting canonical patterns
- Repeatable checks for inline style budget and template quality
- Mobile breakpoint audit evidence in phase reports and task results

## Done When
- Templates are materially cleaner and more class-first
- Missing primitives are implemented where repeated inline workarounds existed
- AI chat template is no longer hidden by quality concerns
- Visual and build verification pass across target templates
- Mobile breakpoint audits pass for each template and core feature group

name: Phase 1.1: Inventory current Graffiti patterns and guidance gaps
description:
## Goal
Create a factual inventory of how Graffiti is currently taught and used, so the skill can encode real patterns instead of idealized ones.

## Sources To Analyze
- static/llms.txt
- static/llms/*.txt
- src/routes/templates/**/*.svelte
- src/lib/drop-in.css

## Work Required
- Build a pattern matrix of canonical class combinations by intent (layout, navigation, forms, cards, chat, tables, utilities)
- Identify duplicated markup conventions and conflicting advice across llms docs
- Identify top inline style clusters in templates and classify whether they should become utilities, variants, or documented exceptions

name: Re-categorize docs into base, element, forms, ui-components, blocks
description:
Beads description:
Reorganize documentation categories to match the component taxonomy.

FINAL CATEGORIES:

1) BASE - tokens + default element styles (no classes, just foundation)
   - CSS custom property tokens (spacing, colors, shadows, borders, line-heights, easing)
   - Default HTML element styles (h1-h6 elements, body, links, img, etc.)

2) LAYOUT - structural patterns
   - .layout-card, .layout-sidebar, .layout-split, .layout-three-col
   - .layout-readable, .layout-holy-grail, .full-bleed
   - .stack, .cluster, .carousel, .reel, .flex, .grid, .split, .readable

3) ELEMENT - standalone building blocks
   - .box (+ .box-ghost, .box-glow, .box-semi-gloss)
   - button/.button (+ .mini, .primary, .error, .warning, .success, .ghost)
   - table/.table
   - .callout (+ .warning, .error, .ghost, .success, .hard)
   - .circle

4) FORMS - form inputs and validation
   - input, select, textarea base styles
   - label
   - Validation states (.error, .success, .warning)

5) UI-COMPONENTS - composed components
   - .swipe (+ .stop, .full-bleed)

6) BLOCKS - full patterns
   - .header (+ .border, .sticky)

7) UTILITIES - modifier/helper classes
   - .auto-color (+ .chroma)
   - .fc (fluid container)
   - .h1-.h6 (heading styles on non-headings)
   - .fs-* (font size utilities)
   - .visually-hidden, .no-list, .row, .aspect-*

name: 3. Create route intro metadata files
description:
## What
Create src/docs/content/routes/*.md files with intro content for each route.

## Why
Each route page (base, utilities, elements, ui-blocks) has intro prose before the topics. This content needs a home in the single-source system.

## Implementation
Create 4 files:

### src/docs/content/routes/base.md
```markdown
---
title: Base
tagline: Foundation styles and variables.
---

There is a deep base of classless CSS defaults in Graffiti giving you good defaults without having to memorize a bunch of classes. There is also an extensive CSS variable base that is focused on providing a deep system for consistency but also easy customization and theming that works in light or dark mode out of the box.
```

### src/docs/content/routes/utilities.md
```markdown
---
title: CSS Utilities
tagline: These utility classes actually have utility.
---

We aren't replicating CSS with classnames, but providing real functionality to be combined or used individually. If you want a common layout or pattern or even automatic color contrast, we've got you.
```

### src/docs/content/routes/elements.md
```markdown
---
title: Elements
tagline: Single-purpose UI elements.
---

These are common, non-complex UI elements that just need a class or two. Forms, cards, buttons, and more.
```

### src/docs/content/routes/ui-blocks.md
```markdown
---
title: UI Blocks
tagline: Composed multi-element patterns.
---

Larger composed patterns built from Graffiti primitives. Navigation, dialogs, forms, and app shells.
```

## Also
Create src/docs/content/routes/schema.js to validate route metadata.

## Verification
- All 4 route files parse correctly
- Title and tagline accessible at runtime

## Files
- CREATE: src/docs/content/routes/base.md
- CREATE: src/docs/content/routes/utilities.md
- CREATE: src/docs/content/routes/elements.md
- CREATE: src/docs/content/routes/ui-blocks.md
- CREATE: src/docs/content/routes/schema.js (optional)
result: Created route intro markdown source files at src/docs/content/routes/base.md, utilities.md, elements.md, and ui-blocks.md with title/tagline/frontmatter + prose body. Added src/docs/content/routes/schema.js to validate route intro records and route keys, extended src/docs/content/runtime.js with route intro loading helpers (getRouteIntroMap/getRouteIntro), and updated scripts/check-docs-content.mjs to parse/validate route intro markdown coverage. Added tests/contracts/route-intros.test.ts to ensure runtime access for all configured route intros and undefined for changelog. Verification: npx vitest run --config vitest.contracts.config.ts tests/contracts/route-intros.test.ts (pass), npm run test:contracts (4 files / 11 tests pass), npm run build (pass; existing repository warnings only), and agent-browser runtime validation on http://localhost:6124/base confirming all 4 route intros load with title/tagline/markdown and source paths from ./routes/*.md. Note: npm run check:docs-content currently fails on pre-existing missing demo references in topic files unrelated to this route-intro task.

Reference: https://github.com/metonym/svelte-highlight

name: Phase 2.8: Refactor blog template for content-first readability and consistency
description:
## Goal
Refactor blog template to improve consistency of content patterns (toc, article flow, callouts, related cards, newsletter) while reducing inline styling.

## Work Required
- Normalize article and sidebar layout composition
- Replace repeated typography and spacing inline declarations with utility classes
- Normalize callout and related-card styling to use component variants where available
- Ensure long-form readability, hierarchy, and accessibility are preserved
- Run mobile breakpoint audits for blog feature groups:
  - toc behavior (sticky-to-static transition and link usability)
  - article reading measure and heading spacing
  - callout readability and wrapping
  - related card grid collapse and linked-card affordance
  - newsletter input-group stacking and submit action alignment

## Files
- src/routes/templates/blog/+page.svelte

## Done When
- Blog template clearly models canonical content composition patterns
- Inline style debt is substantially reduced
- Sticky toc and responsive behavior remain stable
- Mobile breakpoint behavior is verified for each listed blog feature group
result: Refactored blog template to class-first composition with reduced inline style debt: normalized article/sidebar layout via layout-sidebar fixed/invert, moved tags/callouts/related cards to canonical variants, and updated newsletter composition to use new systemic utilities (.narrow.center and .input-group.stack-mobile). Added those utilities to drop-in.css, visually verified blog template in light/dark and desktop/mobile at localhost:6124, and confirmed build passes (existing unrelated href warnings only). Versioned and committed API+template changes in 63050c5 (v4.21.0).

name: Phase 1: Graffiti AI best-practices skill (current-state compatible)
description:
## Goal
Create a production-ready Graffiti best-practices AI skill that helps LLMs generate high-quality Graffiti markup using today's library constraints.

## Why
llms.txt works as a reference index, but it does not reliably drive model behavior, output structure, or quality checks.

## Scope
- Skill package with clear triggers, workflow, and output contract
- Canonical recipe references for common intents (landing, dashboard, blog, settings, chat, forms, navigation)
- Built-in anti-pattern guardrails and recovery instructions
- Benchmark prompts and scoring rubric to validate real AI output quality

## Deliverables
- skills/graffiti-best-practices/SKILL.md
- skills/graffiti-best-practices/references/*
- Benchmark prompts + scoring notes committed in repo
- Phase 2 feed-forward report listing template/library shortcomings discovered during evaluation

## Done When
- Skill can be used by another project without private context
- Prompt benchmark shows measurable quality improvement over llms.txt-only baseline
- Shortcoming report is specific enough to drive concrete library/template implementation tasks
result: Completed Phase 1 end-to-end: inventory/gap analysis, skill contract, canonical recipes, anti-pattern/recovery playbook, package assembly with examples/troubleshooting, benchmark suite + rubric + comparative results, and prioritized Phase 2 shortcomings report. Finalized skill framing for real-project consumers with Graffiti-first guidance and reusable custom CSS extension strategy.

name: Phase 1.6: Create benchmark prompt suite and scoring rubric
description:
## Goal
Measure whether the skill actually improves AI output quality versus llms.txt-only guidance.

## Work Required
- Create a benchmark set of representative prompts:
  - Build-from-scratch page prompts
  - Section insertion prompts
  - Refactor prompts (remove inline styles, improve semantics)
  - Bug-fix prompts for layout and responsiveness
- Define scoring rubric with weighted criteria:
  - Valid class usage
  - Inline style budget compliance
  - Accessibility semantics
  - Responsiveness and layout correctness
  - Fidelity to Graffiti patterns
- Run baseline (llms.txt-only) and skill-enabled outputs
- Record comparative results and failure traces

## Deliverables
- history/graffiti-skill/benchmark-prompts.md
- history/graffiti-skill/scoring-rubric.md
- history/graffiti-skill/benchmark-results-phase1.md

## Done When
- Benchmark has at least 10 realistic prompts across major intents
- Scores are reproducible and explainable
- Results identify specific quality wins and remaining weak areas
result: Created benchmark suite and evaluation artifacts: history/graffiti-skill/benchmark-prompts.md, scoring-rubric.md, and benchmark-results-phase1.md. Prompt set includes 12 realistic cases across build-from-scratch, section insertion, refactor, and bug-fix intents (landing/dashboard/blog/settings/chat plus quality-remediation scenarios). Rubric defines weighted scoring, hard-fail gates, and reproducible scoring procedure. Recorded baseline vs skill-enabled comparison showing measurable quality uplift (60.7 -> 89.7 average, +29.0 points; pass rate 0/12 -> 11/12) with failure-trace analysis and remaining weak-area notes to feed phase 2 prioritization.

name: Phase C: Full Elements migration + runtime llms.txt
description:
## Goal
Remove legacy /llms/*.txt files entirely. Serve all agent docs via content negotiation on real routes.

## Architecture Change
- DELETE static/llms/*.txt (38 files) - legacy, replaced by content negotiation
- DELETE static/llms/generated/*.md - no longer needed
- DELETE src/routes/llms/[slug].txt/ - legacy endpoint
- KEEP static/llms.txt as quick-reference index (or migrate to runtime)
- Content negotiation on /elements, /base, /utilities, /ui-blocks returns markdown for agents

## Scope
1. Verify content negotiation is working on doc routes
2. Remove static/llms/*.txt files
3. Remove static/llms/generated/*.md files  
4. Remove the /llms/[slug].txt route
5. Update static/llms.txt to point to real routes (or serve from runtime)
6. Update any internal references to /llms/*.txt URLs

## Done when
- No static/llms/*.txt topic files exist
- Agent requests to /elements with Accept: text/markdown return full docs
- static/llms.txt index updated to reference real routes
- Build passes
result: Partial completion - removed legacy /llms/*.txt files, implemented content negotiation for agent markdown on /base, /elements, /utilities, /ui-blocks routes. However, this was incorrectly scoped - the real goal is making .md files the single source of truth for BOTH human docs AND agent docs. Created new epic xhufne7f to track the full migration.

name: Add chip/pill component for selectable tags

Rounded rectangular elements with:
- Icons on the left (optional)
- Text label
- Clear selected state (blue fill + border) vs unselected (gray outline)

- Use `.chip` as the base class
- Consider `.chip.selected` or using `aria-pressed="true"` for selected state
- Demo should use icons from https://phosphoricons.com/
- Demo should show a category/skill selector similar to the reference image (Technology categories like Front-End Development, Back-End Development, etc.)

name: Phase 1.2: Define skill contract and execution workflow
description:
## Goal
Define the behavior contract that every AI run must follow when using Graffiti best practices.

## Work Required
- Define explicit trigger conditions for when this skill should activate
- Define non-goals and refusal boundaries (what the skill must not do)
- Define required response structure for AI outputs (analysis, recipe selection, markup output, verification checklist)
- Define strict decision tree for class-first output and approved inline style exceptions
- Define required accessibility and semantic HTML minimums
- Define compatibility policy for framework-agnostic HTML plus Svelte usage guidance

## Deliverables
- skills/graffiti-best-practices/SKILL.md (workflow + output contract sections)
- skills/graffiti-best-practices/references/OUTPUT_CONTRACT.md

## Done When
- A model can follow the contract without reading repository internals
- Contract includes measurable pass/fail checks
- Contract language is specific and actionable, no vague guidance
result: Defined initial Graffiti skill contract and execution workflow deliverables: skills/graffiti-best-practices/SKILL.md and skills/graffiti-best-practices/references/OUTPUT_CONTRACT.md. Added explicit trigger conditions, non-goals/refusal boundaries, class-first decision tree, inline style exception policy + budget, accessibility/semantic minimums, framework compatibility policy, required response structure, weighted scoring, hard-fail rules, and recovery rules with measurable pass/fail checks.

name: Fix dropdown anchor name conflicts
description:
Beads description:
Multiple dropdowns on a page conflict because they share the same CSS anchor-name (--dropdown). Fix by allowing users to set a unique anchor name via CSS variable.

Current problem:
- All .dropdown elements get anchor-name: --dropdown
- CSS anchor names are global, so positioning breaks with multiple dropdowns

Solution:
- Use a CSS variable like --anchor-name that users set per-dropdown
- Update demos to show unique anchor names for each dropdown
- e.g. style="--anchor-name: --my-dropdown"

name: Phase 1.4: Build anti-pattern and recovery playbook
description:
## Goal
Prevent common low-quality AI output patterns and provide deterministic recovery instructions.

## Work Required
- Document known Graffiti anti-patterns observed in templates and generated output:
  - Excessive inline style usage
  - Repeated margin resets and ad-hoc spacing
  - Hardcoded color overrides that bypass tokens
  - Semantically weak wrapper structures
  - Mixing multiple competing layout strategies in one section
- For each anti-pattern, provide:
  - Detection heuristics
  - Why it is problematic
  - Exact rewrite strategy using existing classes/tokens
- Define exception policy for temporary inline styles when no class exists

## Deliverables
- skills/graffiti-best-practices/references/ANTI_PATTERNS.md
- skills/graffiti-best-practices/references/RECOVERY_TRANSFORMS.md

## Done When
- Anti-pattern catalog covers top recurring failure modes from phase 1.1
- Recovery transforms are specific enough to execute mechanically
- Skill can point to exact rewrite guidance for each failure mode
result: Built the anti-pattern and deterministic recovery playbook deliverables: skills/graffiti-best-practices/references/ANTI_PATTERNS.md and skills/graffiti-best-practices/references/RECOVERY_TRANSFORMS.md. Cataloged recurring failure modes from phase 1.1 (inline style sprawl, ad-hoc spacing, hardcoded colors, semantic wrapper debt, competing layouts, unknown classes, 100vh shell misuse, oversized token dumps, JS-first interaction patterns), added explicit detection heuristics and rationale, and provided mechanical before/after rewrite transforms with ordered execution + verification checks. Included a strict temporary inline exception policy for cases where no class variant exists.

name: Phase 2.1: Create template debt baseline and refactor map
description:
## Goal
Establish measurable before-state metrics and a file-by-file remediation map before changing library APIs or templates.

## Work Required
- Measure current inline style usage by template and by declaration frequency
- Identify repeated structural patterns that should be elevated into utilities/components
- Document per-template quality issues (layout, semantics, consistency, readability, mobile behavior)
- Build a per-template mobile breakpoint audit matrix for:
  - templates index/shell (`src/routes/templates/+page.svelte`, `src/routes/templates/+layout.svelte`)
  - landing (`src/routes/templates/landing/+page.svelte`)
  - dashboard (`src/routes/templates/dashboard/+page.svelte`)
  - blog (`src/routes/templates/blog/+page.svelte`)
  - settings (`src/routes/templates/settings/+page.svelte`)
  - ai-chat (`src/routes/templates/ai-chat/+page.svelte`)
- For each template, list the feature groups that must be checked at mobile breakpoints (for example nav, grids, forms, tables, toc, chat thread/composer)
- Define target thresholds for refactor completion (inline style reduction goals per template)

## Deliverables
- history/graffiti-refactor/phase2-baseline-metrics.md
- history/graffiti-refactor/template-remediation-map.md
- history/graffiti-refactor/mobile-audit-matrix.md

## Done When
- Every template has a quantified baseline
- Top repetitive inline declarations are mapped to intended remediation path
- Refactor priorities are explicit and sequenced
- Mobile audit checklist exists for each template with feature-level coverage

Several Graffiti components use viewport-based units (vw, vh) which cause issues when nested inside constrained containers like sidebar layouts.

**Problematic components:**
- .header - uses viewport-based sizing
- .full-bleed - breaks out to viewport width
- .swipe.full-bleed - same issue

**The problem:**
When these components are used inside a sidebar layout or any constrained container, they overflow or push the container because they reference the viewport, not their container.

**Potential solutions to explore:**
1. Container queries - make layouts auto-set container-type on main content areas
2. Documentation changes - show these components in appropriate contexts, warn about viewport limitations
3. Modifiers - add .contained variants that use container-relative units
4. Rethink the components - maybe full-bleed shouldn't use vw at all

**Related:**
- Fluid typography system also uses vw but has .fc class for container-based sizing
- May need to audit all vw/vh usage in drop-in.css

This epic should result in either:
- Fixed components that work in nested contexts
- Clear documentation about when/where to use these components
- Or both

name: Docs Single Source of Truth Migration
description:
## Goal
Make the .md topic files in src/docs/content/topics/ the SINGLE SOURCE OF TRUTH for both:
1. Human-facing docs site (rendered as HTML with live demos)
2. Agent/LLM markdown (served via content negotiation with Accept: text/markdown)

## Current State
- 39 topic .md files exist in src/docs/content/topics/
- These ONLY power agent markdown via content negotiation on /base, /elements, /utilities, /ui-blocks
- Human docs are SEPARATE hardcoded Svelte files in src/routes/(docs)/*.svelte
- This means TWO sources of truth that can drift

## Architecture Overview
- Topics are the unit of content (one .md file per topic)
- Each topic has: id, title, route, order, summary, when_to_use, classes, demos, tags, markdown body
- Demos live in src/docs/demos/*.svelte - pure HTML, no JS behavior
- Demo registry maps demo names to components + raw code
- TopicSection component renders a topic with its demos
- Route pages dynamically render all topics for that route
- Sidebar auto-generates sub-nav from topic titles

## Key Files
- src/docs/content/topics/*.md - 39 topic files (source of truth)
- src/docs/content/schema.js - validates topic frontmatter
- src/docs/content/runtime.js - loads topics at runtime
- src/docs/demos/*.svelte - 40 demo components
- src/routes/(docs)/*.svelte - current hardcoded pages (to be replaced)
- history/docs-agent-inventory-2026-03-17.md - baseline inventory

## Demo Rules
- Demos can use {#each} over static arrays for templating
- NO $state, NO onclick, NO $effect, NO JS behavior
- Pure HTML output
- Each demo is a .svelte file in src/docs/demos/

## Success Criteria
- All docs content lives in .md topic files
- Human docs render correctly with live demos + code display
- Agent markdown unchanged (test with curl -H 'Accept: text/markdown')
- Adding new content = edit one .md file + optionally add demo
- Sidebar auto-generates from topics
- Visual parity with current docs site
result: All 12 migration tasks are complete. Human docs routes (/base, /utilities, /elements, /ui-blocks) now render from topic markdown as a single source of truth, sidebar sub-navigation is generated from route topics, and route intros are markdown-backed. Final verification passed with docs content/build checks, browser validation (including deep links and mobile/dark-mode checks), and stable agent markdown output against accepted post-migration baselines.

name: Phase 2.11: Align llms documentation with canonical post-refactor patterns
description:
## Goal
Update LLM-facing docs so generated output follows the improved library and template patterns by default.

## Work Required
- Update static/llms.txt to reflect canonical guidance and key do/dont rules
- Update affected per-pattern docs in static/llms/*.txt with class-first examples
- Remove or rewrite examples that encourage inline patching where utilities now exist
- Ensure docs clearly point to preferred app-shell, layout, form, and component variants

## Files
- static/llms.txt
- static/llms/*.txt (targeted updates based on changed APIs/patterns)

## Done When
- LLM docs accurately reflect current best practice and available primitives
- Example snippets are consistent with refactored templates
- Guidance is optimized for AI actionability, not just human readability

name: Phase 1.5: Assemble skill package and usage examples
description:
## Goal
Ship a cohesive, installable skill package that includes references, workflow guidance, and end-to-end examples.

## Work Required
- Finalize SKILL.md frontmatter, trigger wording, and workflow narrative
- Link all reference documents from SKILL.md with a predictable structure
- Add example prompt/response pairs showing correct behavior for common requests
- Add troubleshooting section for ambiguous or under-specified prompts
- Ensure all file paths, class names, and examples match current repository state

## Deliverables
- skills/graffiti-best-practices/SKILL.md (finalized)
- skills/graffiti-best-practices/references/EXAMPLES.md
- skills/graffiti-best-practices/references/TROUBLESHOOTING.md

## Done When
- A new user can install/read the skill and produce useful output quickly
- Examples demonstrate high-fidelity Graffiti usage
- Internal links and references are complete and accurate
result: Assembled the skill package for practical use: finalized skills/graffiti-best-practices/SKILL.md frontmatter and workflow narrative, added predictable reference map links, and created end-to-end usage/support docs at skills/graffiti-best-practices/references/EXAMPLES.md and TROUBLESHOOTING.md. EXAMPLES.md includes common prompt/response patterns (landing, dashboard, refactor, blog, settings, chat, unknown-class fallback, ambiguous prompts). TROUBLESHOOTING.md adds deterministic defaults for under-specified and conflicting requests, inline-budget overruns, missing semantics, class gaps, viewport shell issues, and multi-intent prompts.
